summary: Graham argues that startup investing is entering a period of major structural change driven by two forces: startups are becoming cheaper to start, and entrepreneurship is becoming a mainstream career path. While founders will retain more equity and control, the total number of successful startups will increase significantly, meaning top investors can still make more money even as individual deal terms shift in founders' favor.
key_points: The shift from 2 career paths (job or grad school) to 3 (including startups) is a once-in-a-generation social change still in its early stages
The 'rule of thumb' that only 15 companies per year become really successful is not a cosmological constant—it could grow to 50 or 100 as more good founders start companies
The startup landscape will shift from an obelisk shape to a pyramid: slightly wider at the top but much wider at the bottom due to more early failures
Series A investors are increasingly at odds with founders by forcing companies to take more money than needed, creating pent-up opportunity for VCs willing to break ranks
The biggest unexploited opportunity is angel-sized investments made with 24-hour decision timelines, since fundraising costs founders dearly in lost momentum
money_quotes: There are still a lot of people who'd make great founders who never end up starting a company. You can see that from how randomly some of the most successful startups got started. So many of the biggest startups almost didn't happen that there must be a lot of equally good startups that actually didn't happen.
High returns don't come from investing at low valuations. They come from investing in the companies that do really well.
Investors make more money as founders' bitches than their bosses.

You can't fight market forces forever. Over the last decade we've seen the percentage of the company sold in series A rounds creep inexorably downward. 40% used to be common. Now VCs are fighting to hold the line at 20%. But I am daily waiting for the line to collapse.
If you want to find new opportunities for investing, look for things founders complain about. Founders are your customers, and the things they complain about are unsatisfied demand.
key_insight: As startups become cheaper and more common, the power balance shifts permanently toward founders, and the investors who will win are those who adapt to serve founders rather than control them.
